tip: You can create an analysis template from any existing analysis. Go to Analysis Settings → Template tab → Create Template. Templates can then be launched for new samples with one click.
Analysis templates can be configured to auto-launch when a VCF is uploaded based on the enrichment kit or sample name. Set this up in the template settings under 'Auto Launch Config'.
Press the DELETE key to remove selected nodes in the analysis editor (when you're not focused in a text field).
Tick the checkbox for variants in the analysis grid, then use 'Create Selected Child' to instantly create a new node filtered to just those rows.
Each analysis node has a built-in help page. Click the book icon on any node to see documentation for what it does and how to configure it.
You can clone an existing analysis to use as a starting point. There's a 'Clone' button in Analysis Settings.
Analysis settings let you lock an analysis to prevent accidental changes. Useful once you've finalised a workflow or closed a patient case to archive it
The analysis editor keeps an audit log of every node change. Check Analysis Settings → Audit Log tab if you want to see who changed what and when.
The Trio Wizard lets you quickly set up a trio analysis (mother/father/proband) with a guided step-by-step interface. Find it from the VCF/cohort page.
The Quad Wizard extends trio analysis to include a sibling. Handy for recessive inheritance filtering with internal controls.
In the analysis grid, you can filter to any column by using the FilterNode. Filters are case-insensitive and support AND/OR logic across columns.
The node counts that appear below nodes are customisable via Settings → Node Counts tab
The MOI (Mode of Inheritance) node uses zygosity and GenCC gene/disease associations to filter variants by inheritance pattern. No manual gene list required.
The Phenotype node accepts HPO, OMIM, and MONDO terms to filter variants to genes associated with specific diseases or phenotypes.
The Conservation node lets you filter variants by evolutionary conservation scores. Combine it with Effect for tighter pathogenicity filtering.
You can filter variants by whether they're tagged. The Tag node can show only tagged, only untagged, or tagged with specific tags
The Classifications node lets you search for variants that have existing classifications in your lab or visible shared labs.
The Gene List node supports PanelApp UK + Australia panels. Search for a panel by name and it automatically loads the gene list.
The Venn node lets you combine (union), intersect, or subtract variant sets from two parent nodes. Great for comparing sample groups.
You can create a sub-cohort from any cohort. Useful for splitting a large study group into smaller, more specific cohorts for analysis.
The Cohort node can show per-sample het/hom counts in the grid. Use this to quickly see zygosity across all cohort members for each variant.
Download an annotated CSV or VCF directly from any VCF or sample page. The file is generated in the background so downloads are fast.
You can customise the sample name shown in the analysis grid. Set it to display the patient name for easier identification (Sample page → edit sample label).
Sample pages show a graph of chromosomal variant distribution. Useful for spotting sample-level quality issues or unusual patterns.
The Somalier integration can calculate relatedness between samples. Check for unexpected duplicates or verifying family relationships from the sample page.
You can link samples to patients and specimens for traceability. Do this from the Sample settings tab.
BED files can be uploaded and used in the Intersection node to filter variants to genomic intervals you care about. Like custom capture regions.
Create a custom column definition to add calculated or formatted fields to any analysis grid. Go to Settings → Custom Columns.
You can search for a variant by genomic position, HGVS notation, dbSNP rsID, gene symbol, or COSMIC ID. The search bar handles all of these.
Use the prefix 'an' followed by a number in search to jump directly to an analysis by ID (e.g. 'an42').
Searching for a contig or chromosome name like 'chrX' takes you to a dedicated genome build/contig page.
You can manually enter a novel variant through Search → and create + annotate it if it doesn't exist in the database yet
Variant tags are lifted over between genome builds. A tag you apply in GRCh37 analysis will show up in GRCh38 views of the same allele.
You can view all of your variant tags across every analysis in one place from the Variant Tags grid.
Tag colour schemes can be shared across your lab. Create a colour collection in Settings → Tags and share it with your group.
The Variant Details page shows 'Nearby Variants' with tag and classification counts. Great for spotting if a neighbouring variant has already been classified.
Variant Details shows all other samples in your database that carry the same variant. Filterable by zygosity.
The Gene Symbol page has a hotspot graph showing where classifications cluster across the transcript. Click through to see the underlying records.
Gene Symbol pages show GenCC gene/disease relationships with evidence levels. Click to expand the full list including MOI and source.
You can download all classifications for a gene as a CSV from the Gene Symbol page → Classifications tab.
The Gene Wiki lets you add lab-wide notes to any gene. These notes appear across all analysis grids and variant pages for that gene.
The Variant Wiki works the same way as Gene Wiki but is specific to a single variant. Useful for documenting known artefacts or interpretations.
You can search for Ensembl Gene versions like 'ENSG00000243646.11'. It takes you straight to the gene page.
The annotation descriptions page documents every column available in the grids and what it means. Find it under Annotation → Descriptions.
MANE transcripts are indicated and selected as the default on variant detail pages and during classification. Look for the MANE badge on transcripts.
On the variant details page, you can toggle between genome builds if the variant exists in multiple builds. The switcher only shows builds where it's actually present.
Intergenic variants are supported. You can still see limited annotation for variants that fall outside gene boundaries.
The Classification Dashboard shows pathogenicity distribution, discordance summaries, and per-gene classification counts for your lab.
Discordance Reports highlight variants where your lab and another lab have conflicting pathogenicity calls. Review and triage them from the Discordance Reports page.
The Activity feed shows a timeline of recent classification changes across your lab. Great for staying up to date with team activity.
You can create a classification directly from an HGVS string. Useful when you want to classify a variant you haven't uploaded yet.
Condition text in classifications is automatically matched to MONDO, OMIM, and HPO terms. Check the Condition Matchings page to review and fix any unresolved terms.
Classifications support file attachments. Upload PDFs, images, or supporting documents directly to a classification record.
The Candidate Search feature can find existing classifications across your database that might be relevant to a sample you're currently analysing.
You can bulk import classifications from a CSV or Excel file. Go to Classifications → Import Upload to check the expected format and upload.
Export your classifications as a REDCap-compatible CSV for research database integration.
ClinVar export is managed per-lab. Once configured, you can prepare, review, and submit ClinVar batches directly from the ClinVar Export Summary page.
The Classifications Groupings page organises records by allele or gene. Useful for getting a high-level picture of what's been classified.
Ontology term pages show all classifications and patients linked to that term. Useful for finding relevant prior work when classifying a phenotype-associated variant.
Patients can have HPO/OMIM/MONDO phenotype terms attached. These are used by the Phenotype node to filter variants in linked analyses.
You can bulk import patient records from CSV. Download the template first and include phenotype terms, DOB, sex, and specimen info.
The QC Coverage page shows gene-level coverage statistics across sequenced samples. Useful for checking if a gene of interest is well covered in your cohort.
Somalier ancestry analysis is shown on sample pages. It classifies predicted ancestry based on variant patterns across the genome.
Mutational signature analysis shows SNP transition/transversion patterns for a sample. Accessible from the sample page.
The Classification report generates a formatted summary of a classification record that can be shared or printed. Find it in the classification actions menu.
Hover over any annotation column header in a grid for a tooltip explaining what that column means. No need to look it up separately.
User settings let you set a default genome build. So every page defaults to your preferred build without having to switch each time.
Initial group permissions in User Settings control how your newly uploaded data is shared by default. Set read access for your lab so colleagues can see your samples immediately.
IGV integration lets you configure path replacements so that BAM files can be opened in your local IGV directly from the variant page.
You can view the full analysis audit log to see every node configuration change. Great for reproducing an analysis or tracking down when a filter was changed.
The Annotation Versions page shows exactly which version of gnomAD, ClinVar, VEP etc. was used for each annotation run.
The Gene Grid combines gene annotation from multiple sources (GenCC, PanelApp, OMIM, gnomAD constraint) into one comparative view.
You can filter the Samples list by variant type (SNV, indel, SV) to quickly find samples containing specific variant categories.
The Upload page shows VCF processing status in real time. You can monitor annotation progress after uploading a file.
The Analysis grid export includes the date and node version in the filename so you can easily track which version of your analysis produced each download.
The variant annotation history tab shows how annotation values for a variant have changed between annotation runs over time.
You can view the Allele page (prefix 'a' + allele ID) to see a variant unified across genome builds, with liftover coordinates and all associated classifications.
The Karyomapping view lets you visually map inherited chromosomal regions in complex family scenarios.
Sample pages show the full upload and annotation history trail. Useful for understanding if a sample was re-annotated or re-imported.
The Cohort Hotspot page shows which genes are most frequently variant in your cohort. Great for spotting systematic issues or shared disease genes.
You can group samples into a Pedigree and use the Pedigree node in analysis to filter by inheritance across a multi-generation family.
Structural variant annotation includes gnomAD SV frequencies and RepeatMasker overlap. Check the variant details page for SVs like DEL, DUP, and INV.
The 'Nearby Classifications' section on the variant page looks across genome builds. So a GRCh37 classification will show up when viewing the GRCh38 equivalent.
Variant tags can be added or removed directly from the variant detail page. You don't have to be in an analysis to tag a variant.
The server status page shows system health, database statistics, and activity details. Useful for admins checking annotation pipeline status.
You can search for EnrichmentKit names in the global search. Takes you straight to the kit's page showing associated samples.
